original_abstract: The second part of Sir 6:18-37 (vv. 23-31) is a continuation of the pericope’s first part (vv. 18-22). Unlike the first part devoted to wisdom formation, however, the second part focuses on yielding to wisdom and its consequences. Sir 6:23-31 may be divided into three sections. The first one (vv. 23-25) is characterized by the deployment of images connected with servitude, the second one (vv. 26-28) employs the imagery of hunting, whilst the third section (vv. 29-31) returns to the slavery imagery of part one. The second part of Sir 6:18-37 thus has a concentric structure with vv. 6:26-28 as its centre. The fragments framing the central part evince the development of the sage’s thought: while section one (vv. 23-25) concentrates only on yielding to wisdom, of giving up on personal freedom and of the hardships the search for wisdom entails, section three in its allusions to section one depicts the glorious future awaiting those willing to make an effort to acquire wisdom.To acquire wisdom, it is necessary to yield to it fully, the way a slave does to his master. A failure to do so prevents one from the acquisition of wisdom. The second prerequisite is a persistent search for wisdom that should encompass all spheres of life. The sage also indicates the fruits the search for wisdom brings, which include rest, joy, and happiness that wisdom imparts to all those that attain it. In the concluding section Sirach presents the highest distinctions available to those who make an effort and acquire wisdom.
humanized_abstract: The second part of Sir 6:18-37 (vv. 23-31) continues the pericope. While the first part is dedicated to the forming of wisdom, the second part is dedicated to surrendering to wisdom and its consequences. The composition of Sir 6:23-31 is as follows. The first part (vv. 23-25) uses the slavery imagery, the second part (vv. 26-28) uses the hunting imagery, and the third part (vv. 29-31) again uses the slavery imagery. The second part of the composition of the second part of Sir 6:18-37 in the form of a concentric structure with the centre in vv. 6:26-28 shows the development of the thought of the sage. While the first part (vv. 23-25) is dedicated only to surrendering to wisdom, to slavery before wisdom, and to the difficulties that one has to face while searching for wisdom, the third part refers to the first part and describes in allusions the prospects of those who will make an effort to obtain wisdom. The first condition for the obtaining of wisdom is surrender to it, as a slave surrenders to his master. Without this surrender there is no wisdom. The second condition is that the seeking of wisdom should be continuous in all aspects of life. The fruits of the seeking of wisdom are rest, joy and happiness that wisdom will bring to those who obtain it. In the final part of the pericope the sage lists all the possible honours that people can obtain if they will make an effort to obtain wisdom.
